note: Best first stop for a complete Florentine 1480s ensemble. Good for seeing how the layers work together.

links.title: Elena’s Threads: 1480s Florentine Gown
links.desc: Complete ensemble notes with camicia, gamurra, giornea, girdle, reta, and shoes.

links.title: Festive Attyre: Diary of a 1480s Florentine Gown
links.desc: Process notes for a reversible gamurra, giornea, sleeves, and accessories.

links.title: Sophie Stitches: Italy, Gamurra & Giornea 1470s–90s
links.desc: Useful collection of terminology, image inspiration, and pattern/tutorial links.

links.title: YouTube: Dressing up a Florentine Lady 1480–90s
links.desc: Visual dressing sequence for seeing the order of layers.

note: Use these for the washable linen base layer. Earlier/later chemise tutorials are useful construction analogues, but label them as modern reconstruction aids.

links.title: Festive Attyre: How to Make an Easy Italian Chemise
links.desc: Simple rectangular construction tutorial; good first sewing project.

links.title: Elena’s Threads: 1480s Ensemble, Camicia
links.desc: Pleated camicia made for a 1480s Florentine gown, with documentation notes.

links.title: Studio Loperyn PDF: A Woman’s Late 15th Century Italian Camicia
links.desc: Detailed SCA documentation focused specifically on late 15th-century Italian camicia construction.

links.title: YouTube: I Made a 15th / 16th Century Chemise
links.desc: Video build of a camicia/shift style underlayer.

note: This is the core dress layer for a late Quattrocento Italian look. For hot-weather SCA use, make this in breathable linen, lightweight wool, or silk.

links.title: Elena’s Threads: 1480s Florentine Gamurra
links.desc: Gamurra build notes tied to Ghirlandaio evidence.

links.title: Studio Loperyn PDF: Late 15th C Italian Gamurra
links.desc: Documentation and construction notes for a late 15th-century Italian gamurra.

links.title: Margo Anderson: Italian Lady’s Gamurra pattern
links.desc: Commercial pattern for a 1490–1530 gamurra, with bodice and lacing options.

links.title: Reconstructing History RH509: 1470s–1500 Florentine Woman’s Outfit
links.desc: Commercial pattern for a Florentine gamurra/commonwoman outfit with tie-on sleeves, closures, camicia, and historical notes.

links.title: YouTube: How to Make a 15th-Century Italian Renaissance Gown
links.desc: Video build of an Italian Renaissance gown often described as a gamurra/gamora.

links.title: Garb for Me: Drafting a Sleeve Pattern for the Mauve Italian Gamurra
links.desc: Useful for detachable/tie-on sleeve thinking.

note: The giornea is the open-sided/tabard-like overdress layer. It is one of the best ways to make a simple gamurra look more late-15th-century Italian.

links.title: Elena’s Threads: 1480s Giornea
links.desc: Research and construction notes for a 1480s Florentine giornea.

links.title: Dawn’s Dress Diary: 1480s Florence, my Giornea
links.desc: Mock-up and construction decisions for a giornea overgown.

links.title: Reconstructing History RH510: 1470s–1500 Florentine Lady’s Overgown
links.desc: Pattern includes closed and open-sided giornea options.

links.title: Margo Anderson: Italian Lady’s Giornea and Cuffietta
links.desc: Commercial giornea and small cap pattern.

note: Good for formal, courtly, civic, or cooler-weather looks. For summer dance/camp use, consider unlined silk or lightweight wool instead of heavy upholstery fabric.

links.title: Margo Anderson: Italian Lady’s Cioppa Modification Package
links.desc: Cioppa supplement designed to work with the gamurra pattern.

links.title: YouTube: Making a 1490s Italian Renaissance Overdress or Cioppa
links.desc: Video build of a 1490s Italian cioppa/overdress.

links.title: Gratzioso: Blue Silk Cioppa and New Sleeves
links.desc: Construction notes for a lighter, dance-friendly cioppa.

links.title: LiveJournal: 15th Century Florentine Cioppa & Gamurra
links.desc: Project diary with gamurra, cioppa, partlet, and sleeve notes.

note: These are highly visible finishing pieces. The Milanese coazzone/trinzale/lenza set is especially distinctive and regional.

links.title: La Bella Perla: Examination of the Coazzone and Trinzale
links.desc: Detailed explanation of the coazzone, trinzale, and lenza system.

links.title: La Bella Donna: Coazzone, Trinzale, & Lenza of 1490s Milan
links.desc: Reconstruction project with notes on the three-part Milanese hairstyle.

links.title: Dawn’s Dress Diary: 1480s Florence, Reta
links.desc: Reta mock-up and construction notes.

links.title: Rowan Tree Workshop: 1490s Italian Reta
links.desc: Late 15th-century Italian reta construction project.

links.title: Elena’s Threads: 1480s Headgear, Reta
links.desc: Gold net reta made for a Florentine ensemble.

links.title: YouTube: Italian Renaissance Hair Taping Tutorial
links.desc: Practical ribbon hair taping technique for Renaissance Italian-inspired hair.

note: Use these for men’s fitted upper-body garments. Some resources are arming doublets, but the fitting logic is useful for farsetto construction.

links.title: YouTube: Italian Doublet Making Time
links.desc: Patterns a 15th-century farsetto/doublet based on the Diego di Cavaniglia garment.

links.title: Fama Leonis: Under Italian Armor, The Arming Doublet
links.desc: Explains the Italian arming doublet/farsetto context and silhouette.

links.title: Anno 1471: How to Sew a Doublet, Part I
links.desc: Step-by-step doublet design and sewing discussion.

links.title: 1500 Stitches: Venetian Nobleman Zupon
links.desc: Venetian man’s doublet/zupon notes with material references.

links.title: YouTube: Medieval Italian Knight’s Doublet, Measurements
links.desc: Measurement-focused video for an Italian knight’s doublet project.

note: Hose are fitted on the body, often cut on the bias. Mock up first, especially if using non-stretch woven wool.

links.title: Handcrafted History: How to Make Medieval Hose
links.desc: Body-measure drafting and fitting guidance for medieval hose.

links.title: The Medieval Tailor’s Assistant PDF: Modelling Joined Hose
links.desc: Joined hose modelling approach from a widely used historical tailoring reference.

links.title: Seven Star Wheel: Medieval Hose Pattern
links.desc: Printable handout for making a medieval hose pattern.

links.title: Rowan Tree Workshop: Men’s Medieval Hose, 12th–15th Century
links.desc: Project notes for cloth-cut hose.

links.title: YouTube: Medieval Men’s Stirrup Hose Sewing Tutorial
links.desc: Video tutorial for roughly 15th/16th-century stirrup hose.

note: Brache are usually hidden, so these are practical medieval underwear resources rather than specifically late-15th-century Italian visual evidence.

links.title: Maille is Riveting: How to Make Medieval Short Braies
links.desc: Pattern and construction notes for shorter braies that work under later hose.

links.title: Maille is Riveting: How to Make Medieval Long Braies
links.desc: Longer braies construction notes.

links.title: Time Travel Garmentry: Making Braies
links.desc: Simple rectangle-based braies drafting method.

links.title: Matsukaze Sewing: A Pair of Transitional Braies
links.desc: Discusses tighter transitional braies suitable for later medieval fitted clothing.

note: These are useful practical add-ons. They may be broader medieval/Renaissance construction resources, so compare the finished shape against your art source.

links.title: Maille is Riveting: How to Make a Medieval Hood
links.desc: Pattern and fitting method for a hood/cappuccio-like layer.

links.title: Angela Clayton: Renaissance Hat / Beret Drafting Notes
links.desc: Useful modern drafting logic for soft Renaissance caps, though later/interpretive.

links.title: Antique Lilac: Renaissance Hats Tutorial
links.desc: Small-scale hat tutorial that can help visualize cap construction mechanics.

links.title: YouTube: DIY Hand Sewing a 16th Century Tudor Linen Apron
links.desc: Later Tudor/Renaissance apron construction; useful as a basic linen apron method, not Italian-specific.

links.title: Loose Threads: One Afternoon Tutorials, Aprons
links.desc: Fast historical-costume apron tutorial.

note: Footwear is the hardest beginner build. For SCA camping, purchased leather shoes plus wooden pattens/overshoes are more realistic than attempting chopines first.

links.title: Regia Anglorum PDF: A Beginner’s Guide to Turnshoes
links.desc: Medieval turnshoe basics, tools, and patterns.

links.title: Annales Historiae: Adventures in Medieval Shoe-Making
links.desc: Accessible medieval shoe-making tutorial.

links.title: A&S: Late 16th C. Venetian Chopines
links.desc: Later Venetian chopine construction lesson; useful for understanding materials and build logic.

links.title: Handcrafted History: Medieval Pattens Step-to-Step
links.desc: Wooden patten/overshoe construction steps.

links.title: Garb Related Chaos: 15th-Century Pattens Documentation
links.desc: Project documentation for making 15th-century pattens.
